heroOverview: Clonorchiasis is a food-borne parasitic infection of the bile ducts caused by Clonorchis sinensis, the Chinese liver fluke, transmitted through the consumption of raw or inadequately cooked freshwater fish. An estimated 15 to 20 million people are infected worldwide, with the overwhelming burden concentrated in China, Korea, Japan, Vietnam, and the eastern Russian Far East. Adult worms colonise the intrahepatic bile ducts, where they can persist for 20 to 30 years, causing progressive biliary inflammation, cholangitis, and biliary fibrosis. The International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) classified Clonorchis sinensis as a Group 1 definite human carcinogen in 2009 because of its strong causal link with cholangiocarcinoma and hepatocellular carcinoma. Like opisthorchiasis, the infection responds well to a single dose of praziquantel when treated before irreversible biliary damage develops, making early detection and dietary change decisive for long-term outcomes.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 15-20 million people infected worldwide; up to 35 million estimated at risk (WHO, 2022)
keyStats.demographics: Adults aged 30-60 years in East Asian riverine communities; prevalence higher in men in most endemic surveys
keyStats.avgAge: Peak prevalence in adults over 30 years who have consumed raw freshwater fish repeatedly for decades
keyStats.globalCases: Highly endemic in China, Korea, Japan, Vietnam, and Siberia; sporadic cases in immigrant communities globally

definition: Clonorchiasis (ICD-10: B66.1) is infection with Clonorchis sinensis, a hermaphroditic trematode (fluke) of the family Opisthorchiidae, responsible for more than 85% of all liver fluke infections in East Asia. C. sinensis adults are leaf-shaped, 10-25 mm long and 3-5 mm wide, and reside in the distal intrahepatic bile ducts, main bile duct, and occasionally the gallbladder and pancreatic duct. The life cycle parallels that of Opisthorchis species: eggs passed in faeces contaminate freshwater, hatch into miracidia that infect freshwater snails (Parafossarulus manchouricus and related species), develop into cercariae, and penetrate the skin or scales of over 80 species of cyprinid fish to form metacercariae. Humans acquire infection by eating raw, dried, smoked, or lightly pickled fish. After oral ingestion, larvae excyst in the duodenum, migrate up the bile duct, and mature into adults within 3 to 4 weeks. Worms survive for 20 to 30 years, continuously laying eggs (approximately 2,000 per pair per day). Heavy worm burdens, defined as more than 500 worms, cause mechanical obstruction and sustained biliary epithelial irritation from fluke secretions, driving periductal inflammation, hyperplasia, and goblet cell metaplasia — the precursor lesion for cholangiocarcinoma. IARC classified C. sinensis as a Group 1 carcinogen in 2009, making clonorchiasis the most oncogenically significant human helminth infection. Management involves infectious disease physicians, gastroenterologists, and hepatobiliary surgeons for complicated or malignant disease.

typesClassification.description: Occurs in immunologically naïve individuals after a large initial infective dose; characterised by fever, right upper quadrant pain, hepatomegaly, and marked eosinophilia developing 3-4 weeks after ingestion.

Fewer than 100 worms; typically asymptomatic or producing only mild dyspepsia and fatigue. Detectable only on stool examination; most prevalent form in regions where small amounts of raw fish are consumed regularly.

Hundreds to thousands of worms causing persistent cholangitis, biliary fibrosis, and intrahepatic stone formation. Found in older adults in hyperendemic areas (parts of southern China and Korea); carries the highest malignant transformation risk.

Bile duct malignancy arising after decades of heavy chronic infection; associated with a 4.8-fold relative risk increase (meta-analysis data). Presents with progressive jaundice, weight loss, and a biliary mass on imaging in the fifth to seventh decade.

primarySymptoms: Right upper quadrant or epigastric abdominal pain, typically a dull pressure or ache under the right rib margin, from bile duct distension and periductal inflammation.
Dyspepsia, bloating, and nausea after fatty meals, reflecting impaired bile secretion and gallbladder dysfunction from fluke-related biliary stasis.
Intermittent jaundice and dark urine, occurring when worm load or biliary stone formation obstructs adequate bile flow into the duodenum.
Fatigue and malaise persisting for months, characteristic of moderate to heavy worm burdens and the associated immune activation.
Fever with chills during episodes of secondary bacterial cholangitis, when bile duct obstruction creates a nidus for ascending bacterial infection.
Hepatomegaly — a palpably enlarged, sometimes tender liver — proportional to worm burden and degree of biliary inflammation.
Anorexia and weight loss in heavy chronic infections, from malabsorption, a catabolic inflammatory state, and reduced appetite.
Peripheral oedema in severe chronic disease with advanced biliary fibrosis and impaired hepatic protein synthesis.
Eosinophilia on blood count — a key laboratory clue, particularly prominent (10-40% eosinophils) during acute infection and larval migration.
Itching (pruritus) from cholestasis — bile salt deposition in the skin from impaired biliary excretion during obstructive episodes.
earlyWarningSigns: Vague intermittent right-sided abdominal discomfort in a person who habitually eats raw or lightly pickled freshwater fish.
Unexplained eosinophilia on a blood count in someone from or having lived in China, Korea, Japan, or Vietnam.
Mild persistent dyspepsia that does not respond to antacids or dietary modification in an East Asian patient.
Subtle jaundice — yellowing of the sclera — detected on routine examination in an older adult from an endemic area.
emergencySigns: Charcot's triad — simultaneous fever above 39°C, right upper quadrant pain, and jaundice — indicating acute bacterial cholangitis requiring emergency antibiotics and biliary drainage.
Reynold's pentad (Charcot's triad plus septic shock and altered consciousness) — a life-threatening surgical emergency.
Sudden severe abdominal pain with peritoneal signs suggesting gallbladder perforation or biliary peritonitis.
Rapidly deepening jaundice with pale stools and dark urine indicating complete biliary obstruction needing urgent endoscopic or surgical decompression.
New painless jaundice with back pain and significant weight loss in a patient with known chronic clonorchiasis — features requiring immediate evaluation for cholangiocarcinoma.

causes.cause: Ingestion of raw, undercooked, or fermented freshwater fish
causes.description: The sole route of human infection. Metacercariae encysted in the muscles and connective tissue of cyprinid fish remain infectious after light smoking, salting, drying, and pickling unless core temperature exceeds 60°C or freezing at -20°C for 7 days is applied. Traditional preparations — raw fish congee, san-nakji pairings, raw carp — are the primary vehicles in China and Korea.

causes.cause: Clonorchis sinensis lifecycle in contaminated freshwater
causes.description: Eggs excreted in faeces of infected humans, cats, and dogs contaminate freshwater bodies. Miracidia infect freshwater snails (Parafossarulus manchouricus, Bithynia and related genera), which shed cercariae that penetrate over 80 cyprinid fish species. The lifecycle is maintained where human or animal faeces enter freshwater and where people eat incompletely processed fish.

causes.cause: Environmental contamination from human and animal reservoirs
causes.description: Domestic animals — particularly cats, dogs, and pigs — also serve as definitive hosts, providing a continuous source of egg contamination in fish ponds, rivers, and irrigation canals. In communities that use fish pond water for agriculture or release untreated sewage, environmental egg concentrations remain high.

Repeated fluke attachment, feeding, and egg deposition damage bile duct epithelium and stimulate periductal fibrosis. Fluke excretory-secretory products induce oxidative DNA damage, promote hyperplasia, inhibit apoptosis, and stimulate abnormal cell proliferation — creating the molecular carcinogenic cascade that leads to cholangiocarcinoma after 10-30 years in heavily infected hosts.

causes.cause: High fish consumption in East Asian communities
causes.description: In southern China and the Korean peninsula, raw freshwater fish dishes are culturally significant and deeply embedded. High per capita consumption rates in riverine communities generate both elevated individual exposure and sustained environmental egg loads from infected human hosts.

riskFactors.factor: Regular consumption of raw or underprocessed freshwater fish
riskFactors.description: The single greatest modifiable risk factor: each meal of raw fish in an endemic area represents a fresh exposure event. Eliminating raw fish consumption eliminates new infections. Cultural preference for raw fish in China, Korea, and Vietnam makes this a substantial public health challenge.

riskFactors.factor: Living in or emigrating from hyperendemic areas
riskFactors.description: Southern China (Guangdong, Guangxi), the Korean peninsula, and parts of Vietnam have prevalence rates of 10-60% in some riverside communities. Immigrants who maintain traditional food practices or return to their home countries carry ongoing risk.

riskFactors.factor: Age over 30 years with long exposure history
riskFactors.description: Worm burden and cumulative biliary damage accumulate over decades. The cholangiocarcinoma risk is highest in patients over 50 who have had heavy chronic infection for 20+ years, explaining why cancer presents most often in the sixth decade.

Men are infected approximately 1.5 to 2 times more often than women in most endemic-area studies, likely because of higher raw fish consumption and occupational exposure to fishing and fish processing.

riskFactors.factor: Concurrent hepatitis B or C infection
riskFactors.description: Clonorchiasis co-existing with hepatitis B or C amplifies hepatocellular carcinoma risk beyond either infection alone. In areas where both are endemic, the combined carcinogenic burden is substantially elevated.
Combined chronic clonorchiasis and heavy alcohol intake creates additive liver stress and is associated with accelerated biliary fibrosis and elevated cancer risk in Korean cohort studies.
Fishing, fish farming, and fish processing workers in endemic areas may have higher exposure through incidental ingestion of contaminated material or consumption of fish on-site.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 30-60 years in southern China, the Korean peninsula, and the Mekong delta of Vietnam
Farming and fishing communities with direct access to freshwater fish in rivers and fish ponds
Men slightly more than women in all major prevalence surveys
Korean and Chinese immigrant communities in North America, Europe, and Australia who eat traditional raw fish dishes
Patients co-infected with hepatitis B or C who face amplified cancer risk
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of clonorchiasis combines a targeted history — specifically asking about raw freshwater fish consumption and geographic exposure to endemic East Asian regions — with parasitological, serological, and imaging investigations. Stool examination for eggs is the cornerstone diagnostic method: Clonorchis eggs (28-35 µm, operculated, with a small posterior knob) are nearly identical to Opisthorchis eggs and cannot be reliably distinguished by morphology alone, making geographic origin critical for species attribution. Formalin-ether concentration (FECT) or Kato-Katz thick smear improves sensitivity from about 55-65% for a single sample to 85-95% with three consecutive specimens. For light infections, a stool antigen ELISA specific for Clonorchis or cross-reactive opisthorchiid antigens offers greater sensitivity than microscopy. Serology (antibody detection by ELISA) supports diagnosis in travellers and in early infection before significant egg shedding; sensitivity is 70-90% but cross-reactions with Opisthorchis, Fasciola, and other flukes reduce specificity. PCR assays applied to stool or serum provide species-level identification where available. Abdominal ultrasound is the primary imaging modality for assessing biliary complications: it demonstrates hyperechoic bile duct walls, dilated intrahepatic ducts, gallbladder wall thickening, biliary sludge, and intrahepatic stones. CT or MRI better characterises the extent of biliary dilation, hepatic fibrosis, and suspicious mass lesions. Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) provides non-invasive high-resolution biliary anatomy. Serum alkaline phosphatase, gamma-GT, and bilirubin are elevated in cholestatic disease. When cholangiocarcinoma is suspected, CA 19-9 and CEA are measured, though neither is specific. Diagnosis in combination with the raw fish exposure history is sufficient to initiate treatment empirically when stool testing confirms compatible eggs in a patient from an endemic region.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies operculated Clonorchis eggs in stool; the standard field-applicable diagnostic test and the basis for determining infection intensity (eggs per gram of faeces).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Three fresh stool samples collected on separate days are each processed into thick smears or concentrated pellets and examined under a light microscope. Results available in hours at an equipped laboratory.

diagnosticTests.test: Stool antigen or serum antibody ELISA
diagnosticTests.purpose: More sensitive than egg microscopy for light infections; antibody tests support diagnosis in travellers or early infection; antigen tests confirm active infection and can be used to monitor treatment response.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A stool or blood sample is sent to a reference laboratory. Results typically available in 1-3 days. Positive antibody results in travellers from endemic regions with compatible history are highly suggestive.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses biliary tract changes — echogenic duct walls, intrahepatic bile duct dilation, biliary sludge, stones — and detects mass lesions concerning for cholangiocarcinoma in chronic disease.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Painless scan with ultrasound gel on the abdomen taking 15-20 minutes, performed fasting for best gallbladder assessment. Recommended as baseline at diagnosis and annually in patients over 40 with heavy infection.

diagnosticTests.test: Complete blood count with eosinophil differential
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects peripheral eosinophilia (typically 10-40% in acute phase), which is a key laboratory indicator prompting investigation for helminth infection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results in 1-2 hours. Eosinophil count falls to normal within 3 months after successful treatment, making it a useful treatment-response marker.

diagnosticTests.test: Liver function tests (alkaline phosphatase, GGT, bilirubin)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies cholestatic and hepatocellular injury from biliary disease; guides severity assessment and monitoring of chronic hepatobiliary complications.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw processed in 1-2 hours; results interpreted in context of symptoms, worm burden, and imaging findings.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides detailed biliary anatomy in complicated disease — strictures, stones, tumour — and guides surgical or endoscopic planning when ultrasound is insufficient.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: MRCP: 30-60 minute MRI scan; no ionising radiation; non-invasive. CT with contrast: faster, 5-10 minutes, uses iodinated contrast agent injected intravenously. Both are performed fasting.

treatmentOverview: Praziquantel is the drug of choice for clonorchiasis, with efficacy confirmed in numerous randomised trials across Chinese and Korean populations. The recommended regimen is a single oral dose of 25 mg/kg three times in one day (total 75 mg/kg) or, for adults, a single dose of 40 mg/kg taken with food. Cure rates range from 79 to 100% depending on the worm burden and definition of cure (egg clearance at 4-6 weeks). Adverse effects — dizziness, headache, nausea, mild abdominal cramping — typically resolve within 24 hours and are managed symptomatically. Albendazole 400 mg twice daily for 7 days is a second-line option, with efficacy of approximately 50-60%. Tribendimidine, available in China, has demonstrated equivalence to praziquantel in Phase 3 trials and is an alternative in that country. Treatment response should be confirmed by stool examination 4-6 weeks after treatment; a repeat course is given if eggs persist. In endemic communities, programmatic mass drug administration with praziquantel is the main public health strategy, recommended alongside health education about cooking fish safely. Biliary complications require additional interventions: bacterial cholangitis needs broad-spectrum antibiotics plus biliary drainage; intrahepatic stones may require ERCP with stone extraction or surgery; and cholangiocarcinoma, if detected, requires multidisciplinary oncological care. Dietary counselling to discontinue raw fish consumption is an essential component of treatment — without behaviour change, re-infection rates approach 60-80% in the first year in hyperendemic areas. Post-treatment surveillance ultrasound every 6-12 months is recommended for patients over 40 with a history of heavy infection because biliary fibrosis and cancer risk persist after parasitological cure.

medicalTreatments.name: Praziquantel (three-dose day regimen)
medicalTreatments.description: The drug of choice: 25 mg/kg given three times in one day (75 mg/kg total) with meals. Causes spastic paralysis and tegumental disruption of flukes. Widely available, inexpensive, and suitable for mass treatment programmes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates of 79-100% in clinical trials across China and Korea; WHO guidelines support this as the first-line regimen for all ages.

medicalTreatments.name: Albendazole (7-day course)
medicalTreatments.description: Benzimidazole alternative given at 400 mg twice daily for 7 days when praziquantel is contraindicated or unavailable. Disrupts microtubule synthesis in the fluke. Also effective against co-infecting soil-transmitted helminths.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 50-60% cure rate for clonorchiasis, lower than praziquantel; useful when treating co-infections.

medicalTreatments.name: Tribendimidine (single oral dose, China)
medicalTreatments.description: A dehydroaminoacetaldehyde anthelmintic licensed in China for clonorchiasis; given as a single dose of 400 mg in adults. Phase 3 data demonstrate non-inferiority to praziquantel with a comparable safety profile.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates of approximately 75-85% in Phase 3 Chinese trials; not widely available outside China and select Asian markets.

medicalTreatments.name: Antibiotics for secondary bacterial cholangitis
medicalTreatments.description: Gram-negative organisms including E. coli, Klebsiella, and Enterococcus commonly cause secondary cholangitis in obstructed bile ducts. Broad-spectrum agents such as ceftriaxone, piperacillin-tazobactam, or ciprofloxacin are used pending cultures, combined with biliary drainage.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical resolution in approximately 80-90% of mild-to-moderate cholangitis episodes with antibiotics plus adequate drainage; severe septic cholangitis requires urgent biliary decompression.

medicalTreatments.name: Ursodeoxycholic acid (adjunctive)
medicalTreatments.description: Used in patients with biliary sludge or intrahepatic stones to reduce biliary cholesterol saturation, improve bile flow, and limit further stone formation and inflammation after praziquantel treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest improvements in biliary sludge and liver enzyme levels in open-label studies; not a substitute for antiparasitic treatment.

surgicalOptions.name: ERCP with stone extraction or stenting
surgicalOptions.description: Endoscopic management of intrahepatic or common bile duct stones and biliary strictures caused by chronic clonorchiasis. Can also be used to obtain biliary cytology when cholangiocarcinoma is suspected.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Technical success rate for stone clearance in common bile duct stones exceeds 85-90% at experienced centres; multiple sessions may be needed for intrahepatic stones.

surgicalOptions.name: Hepatic resection for cholangiocarcinoma
surgicalOptions.description: Potentially curative surgery involving partial hepatectomy with or without bile duct resection and bilioenteric anastomosis, reserved for resectable perihilar or intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma confirmed at specialist evaluation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: R0 resection achieved in 50-70% of operated patients at high-volume centres; 5-year survival approximately 30-40% after curative resection, compared to below 10% with unresectable disease.

surgicalOptions.name: Cholecystectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of the gallbladder in patients with recurrent cholecystitis or symptomatic gallstones related to chronic clonorchiasis-associated biliary stasis and inflammation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy resolves recurrent cholecystitis in approximately 95% of elective cases with a low complication rate.

alternativeTreatments.name: Thorough cooking of freshwater fish (dietary behaviour change)
alternativeTreatments.description: Heating fish to an internal temperature of 60°C for at least 1 minute or freezing at -20°C for 7 days kills all metacercariae. This is the most impactful non-pharmacological intervention, preventing re-infection after treatment and reducing community transmission in endemic areas.

alternativeTreatments.name: Community-based health education and mass drug administration
alternativeTreatments.description: WHO-endorsed combination of annual or biannual praziquantel distribution, food safety education campaigns, and improved sanitation in endemic communities. This approach, used successfully in parts of Korea, has reduced prevalence substantially over decades.

whySeeSpecialist: Uncomplicated clonorchiasis can be treated by a primary care physician or infectious disease physician with praziquantel and dietary counselling. Specialist involvement is warranted when biliary complications develop — cholangitis, stones, biliary strictures — or when cholangiocarcinoma is suspected. A gastroenterologist or hepatologist should be consulted for ERCP, biliary imaging interpretation, and surveillance in high-risk patients. Any patient over 40 with a confirmed history of heavy chronic infection warrants a specialist-led surveillance programme with periodic ultrasound, given the significantly elevated lifetime cancer risk. Cholangiocarcinoma management requires an experienced hepatobiliary surgical team at a cancer centre.
doctorSelectionGuide: For straightforward treatment in endemic areas, district-level infectious disease practitioners and public health facilities dispense praziquantel effectively. For patients in non-endemic countries who have immigrated from East Asia, an infectious disease or travel medicine specialist familiar with parasitic infections is the best starting point. Biliary complications are managed by gastroenterologists with ERCP capabilities. Suspected cholangiocarcinoma should be referred immediately to a specialist hepatobiliary surgery and oncology multidisciplinary team — survival outcomes improve significantly at high-volume centres compared with community hospitals.
preventionStrategies: Always cook freshwater fish to an internal temperature of at least 60°C; boiling, frying, baking, or steaming to this standard kills all metacercariae.
Freeze freshwater fish at -20°C for at least 7 days before eating raw or lightly prepared dishes.
Avoid raw freshwater fish dishes including raw fish congee, marinated carp, and similar preparations in or from endemic regions.
Improve sanitation: use latrines and prevent human or animal faeces from entering waterways or fish ponds used for human consumption.
Participate in mass drug administration programmes where available in hyperendemic areas.
Get a stool test for clonorchiasis if you have lived in or regularly visited endemic areas and eaten raw freshwater fish, particularly if over 40 years old.
lifestyleModifications: Permanently discontinue consumption of raw, fermented, or lightly dried freshwater fish after treatment to prevent re-infection.
Adopt consistently cooked fish preparation and teach food safety practices to household members in endemic areas.
Reduce or eliminate alcohol consumption if you have a history of chronic infection, as alcohol compounds biliary damage and cancer risk.
Keep all follow-up appointments: stool examination at 4-6 weeks post-treatment and annual ultrasound if aged over 40 with heavy infection history.
Inform your physician of any prior clonorchiasis when being assessed for other liver or biliary conditions — your history is clinically relevant for life.

recommended: Fully cooked freshwater fish — nutritious and safe once heated to 60°C throughout
Saltwater fish and shellfish, which do not transmit Clonorchis
A fibre-rich diet supporting regular bowel movements and bile flow
Adequate hydration to support biliary function
avoid: Raw carp, grass carp, or other cyprinid fish in any preparation unless commercially certified parasite-free
Lightly salted, dried, or smoked freshwater fish from endemic regions without verified temperature treatment
Sashimi-style preparations of freshwater fish from China, Korea, or Vietnam
Excess alcohol, which synergises with biliary inflammation in increasing cancer risk

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate exercise is encouraged throughout the course of clonorchiasis. During episodes of acute cholangitis — with fever, jaundice, and abdominal pain — rest and medical treatment take priority. After successful praziquantel treatment and confirmed parasitological cure, there is no need to restrict physical activity. Exercise supporting healthy body weight may reduce the metabolic demands on the liver and support recovery from biliary inflammation.
dailyManagement: Take praziquantel with food to reduce nausea; plan a rest day around the dosing date as dizziness is common.
Return for stool examination 4-6 weeks after treatment to confirm egg clearance; persist with retesting if initial result is equivocal.
Keep a written record of your diagnosis and treatment date — this is important for future medical care, particularly if jaundice, biliary pain, or unexplained weight loss occurs years later.
In endemic settings: commit permanently to cooked freshwater fish, as re-infection after treatment adds cumulative biliary damage.
Arrange annual abdominal ultrasound if over 40 years old with a history of heavy or long-duration infection; do not skip these checks as they can detect cholangiocarcinoma at a curable stage.
prognosis: The prognosis of clonorchiasis is excellent when treated early: praziquantel achieves parasitological cure in approximately 79-100% of patients, symptoms resolve within weeks, and eosinophilia normalises within 1-3 months. In individuals treated before significant biliary damage accumulates, the liver can heal substantially, and cancer risk is reduced compared to untreated patients. However, the prognosis is substantially worse for those with long-standing heavy infection who develop biliary fibrosis or cholangiocarcinoma. Clonorchiasis-related cholangiocarcinoma carries a 5-year survival below 10% when detected at an unresectable stage and approximately 30-40% after complete surgical resection — making early detection through surveillance ultrasound critical. Re-infection after treatment is common in endemic settings, with re-infection rates of 60-80% in the first year among those who continue raw fish consumption. National programmes in Korea, combining periodic screening, praziquantel treatment, and health education, have achieved substantial prevalence reductions over four decades, demonstrating that population-level control is achievable.
recoveryTimeline: Symptoms of acute clonorchiasis typically resolve within 2 to 4 weeks of treatment. Eosinophilia normalises within 1 to 3 months. Eggs clear from the stool within 4 to 6 weeks in most patients, confirmed by follow-up stool examination. Liver enzyme elevations improve over 3 to 6 months after worm eradication. In chronic disease with biliary fibrosis and complications, structural changes may improve partially over months to years but do not fully reverse. Patients with biliary strictures or stones require ongoing specialist monitoring and may need procedural interventions.
complications: Recurrent pyogenic cholangitis — the most common severe complication; repeated bile duct obstruction and bacterial infection causing persistent fever, jaundice, and abdominal pain, leading to intrahepatic stone formation and biliary sepsis.
Cholangiocarcinoma — bile duct cancer with a 4.8-fold relative risk increase in long-standing heavy infection; accounts for the largest proportion of clonorchiasis-related morbidity in endemic areas.

Intrahepatic stones (hepatolithiasis) — bile pigment and cholesterol stones forming in dilated bile ducts, causing recurrent cholangitis and difficult to treat without endoscopic or surgical stone clearance.
Acute pancreatitis — worms or stones obstructing the pancreatic duct; can cause both acute and chronic pancreatic inflammation.

Biliary cirrhosis and portal hypertension — in very heavy long-standing infection; progressive periductal fibrosis extending to hepatic parenchyma causing portal hypertension, ascites, and variceal bleeding.
Hepatocellular carcinoma — particularly in patients co-infected with hepatitis B or C, in whom clonorchiasis amplifies viral carcinogenic effects.

supportResources.description: CDC diagnostic and clinical guidance for clonorchiasis for healthcare providers in non-endemic countries.

supportResources.name: Korean Ministry of Health and Welfare — Parasite Control Programme
supportResources.description: Korea CDC resource on national parasite control efforts including clonorchiasis surveillance and mass treatment data.

sources.title: Lun ZR et al. Clonorchiasis: a key foodborne zoonosis in China. Lancet Infectious Diseases, 2005

confusedWithConditions.name: Opisthorchiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Opisthorchis viverrini and felineus cause an almost identical clinical syndrome via the same fish-borne route; geographic origin distinguishes them — clonorchiasis predominates in East Asia, opisthorchiasis in Southeast Asia and Siberia. Egg morphology alone is insufficient for species differentiation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Fascioliasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Fasciola hepatica is a much larger fluke acquired from eating watercress or aquatic plants, not fish. It causes an acute febrile illness with very high eosinophilia and liver tenderness, and requires triclabendazole rather than praziquantel for treatment.

confusedWithConditions.name: Bacterial cholangitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Primary bacterial cholangitis without an underlying parasitic cause presents similarly — fever, jaundice, right upper quadrant pain — but lacks the raw fish exposure history, egg-positive stool, and eosinophilia of clonorchiasis. Clonorchiasis is often the underlying driver of secondary bacterial cholangitis in endemic areas.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hepatitis B
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hepatitis B causes hepatomegaly and elevated liver enzymes like clonorchiasis, but is transmitted by blood or sexual contact, identified by specific serology, and does not produce bile duct-dwelling worms detectable as eggs in stool.

relatedConditions.name: Opisthorchiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Opisthorchis viverrini is the Southeast Asian counterpart of Clonorchis sinensis; both are fish-borne liver flukes causing biliary disease and cholangiocarcinoma, treated with praziquantel, and classified as Group 1 carcinogens.

relatedConditions.name: Fascioliasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another food-borne trematode infecting the bile ducts, acquired from plants rather than fish, with overlapping biliary pathology but different treatment and epidemiology.

relatedConditions.name: Cholangitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Clonorchiasis is the most common identifiable cause of recurrent pyogenic cholangitis in East Asia; the two conditions frequently coexist and require simultaneous management.

relatedConditions.name: Schistosomiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Co-endemic water-related helminth infection in parts of China and Southeast Asia; shares the praziquantel treatment approach and hepatosplenic complications.

faqs.question: What is clonorchiasis?
faqs.answer: Clonorchiasis is a parasitic infection of the bile ducts caused by the Chinese liver fluke Clonorchis sinensis. People acquire it by eating raw or undercooked freshwater fish. The worms can live in the bile ducts for up to 30 years, causing biliary disease and, in heavy long-term infections, bile duct cancer.

faqs.question: How do you get clonorchiasis?
faqs.answer: Clonorchiasis is acquired by eating freshwater fish containing infective larvae. Raw, lightly salted, dried, smoked, or fermented fish are common sources in East Asia. Drinking water or contact with an infected person does not transmit the infection.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of clonorchiasis?
faqs.answer: Light infections are usually symptom-free. With heavier infections, symptoms include right-sided abdominal pain, indigestion, nausea, fatigue, and intermittent jaundice. Fever and severe abdominal pain with jaundice — Charcot's triad — indicate secondary bacterial cholangitis and require urgent care.

faqs.question: How is clonorchiasis treated?
faqs.answer: Praziquantel is the drug of choice, given as 25 mg/kg three times in one day (total 75 mg/kg) with food. Cure rates are 79-100%. Side effects such as dizziness, nausea, and mild abdominal pain typically last only a few hours. A follow-up stool test at 4-6 weeks confirms cure.

faqs.question: Can clonorchiasis cause cancer?
faqs.answer: Yes. Clonorchis sinensis is classified by IARC as a Group 1 definite human carcinogen because chronic heavy infection causes cholangiocarcinoma (bile duct cancer). People over 40 with a long history of heavy infection should have regular liver ultrasound to detect early cancer.

faqs.question: Where is clonorchiasis most common?
faqs.answer: Clonorchiasis is most common in China, South Korea, Japan, Vietnam, and the Russian Far East. An estimated 15-20 million people worldwide are infected, with the largest burden in southern China and the Korean peninsula.

faqs.question: How is clonorchiasis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Stool microscopy detecting small operculated eggs is the standard test; examining three stool samples on different days improves sensitivity to 85-95%. Blood tests showing eosinophilia support the diagnosis. Abdominal ultrasound assesses biliary complications. Serology can help in travellers or early infections.

faqs.question: How long can clonorchis flukes live in the body?
faqs.answer: Adult Clonorchis sinensis flukes can survive in the human bile ducts for 20 to 30 years without treatment, continuously producing eggs and causing biliary inflammation. This long survival is the main reason cumulative damage and cancer risk increase with age.

faqs.question: What is the difference between clonorchiasis and opisthorchiasis?
faqs.answer: Both are liver fluke infections caused by closely related species and have virtually identical clinical presentations. Clonorchis sinensis predominates in East Asia (China, Korea, Japan); Opisthorchis species are more prevalent in Southeast Asia (Thailand, Laos) and Siberia. Both are treated with praziquantel and both cause cholangiocarcinoma.

faqs.question: Can clonorchiasis be prevented?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cook freshwater fish to at least 60°C throughout, or freeze at -20°C for 7 days before eating. Avoid raw freshwater fish dishes in or from endemic East Asian regions. Improved sanitation to prevent faecal contamination of waterways also reduces transmission at the community level.

faqs.question: Is clonorchiasis contagious?
faqs.answer: No. Clonorchiasis cannot spread directly from person to person. The parasite requires a specific snail and fish as intermediate hosts; human-to-human transmission does not occur. Only eating contaminated fish can infect a new host.

faqs.question: What ultrasound findings suggest clonorchiasis?
faqs.answer: Ultrasound in chronic clonorchiasis typically shows dilated intrahepatic bile ducts with echogenic walls, a thickened or hyperechoic gallbladder wall, biliary sludge, and intrahepatic stones. Mass lesions in the bile duct or liver raise concern for cholangiocarcinoma and need further imaging.

faqs.question: Can clonorchiasis come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes, if raw fish consumption continues. Treatment clears the current worm burden, but re-infection from subsequent raw fish meals is very common in endemic areas, with rates of 60-80% within a year in communities where the dietary habit persists. Avoiding raw freshwater fish permanently is essential.

faqs.question: Who is most at risk of clonorchiasis?
faqs.answer: Adults aged 30-60 who regularly eat raw freshwater fish in China, Korea, Japan, Vietnam, and related immigrant communities are most at risk. Men have slightly higher prevalence than women in endemic surveys. Older adults with decades of heavy exposure face the greatest cancer risk.

faqs.question: What happens in the body during clonorchiasis?
faqs.answer: Adult flukes live in the bile ducts and feed on bile duct epithelium and secretions. Their secretory products damage the duct lining, stimulate inflammation, and drive progressive fibrosis. Eggs accumulate in bile, causing biliary stasis and stone formation. Over decades this environment can transform into bile duct cancer.

faqs.question: Is praziquantel safe for children with clonorchiasis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Praziquantel is used safely in children for clonorchiasis at weight-based dosing. It is generally avoided in infants under 4 years old. Side effects in children are similar to adults — temporary dizziness, nausea, and abdominal discomfort — and are well tolerated in mass treatment programmes.

faqs.question: When should I see a specialist for clonorchiasis?
faqs.answer: See a gastroenterologist or hepatologist if you develop persistent jaundice, fever with jaundice, or severe abdominal pain — signs of biliary complications. Seek urgent specialist evaluation if you are over 40 with a long history of infection and develop unexplained weight loss, back pain, or deepening jaundice, which can indicate cholangiocarcinoma.

faqs.question: How is the cancer risk managed in people with clonorchiasis?
faqs.answer: For patients over 40 with confirmed heavy or long-duration clonorchiasis, annual abdominal ultrasound with liver function tests is recommended to screen for early cholangiocarcinoma. CA 19-9 and CEA tumour markers may be checked alongside imaging. Early-stage cancer is potentially curable with surgery; late-stage is not.

faqs.question: Can clonorchiasis cause problems with the pancreas?
faqs.answer: Yes, though less commonly than biliary complications. Flukes or fluke-related biliary stones can obstruct the pancreatic duct, causing acute pancreatitis. People with unexplained recurrent pancreatitis from an East Asian endemic region should be tested for clonorchiasis.

faqs.question: Is there a national programme to control clonorchiasis?
faqs.answer: South Korea has operated one of the world's longest-running parasite control programmes, combining periodic mass screening, praziquantel treatment, and health education campaigns since the 1970s. Prevalence in Korea fell from over 4% in 1971 to less than 1% by 2020. Similar programmes operate in parts of China.

symptomKeywords: abdominal pain raw fish China Korea
metaDescription: Clonorchiasis is an East Asian liver fluke infection from raw freshwater fish that causes bile duct disease and cancer risk. Learn symptoms and treatment.
